ux_issues.description: In Test 2, the AI called create_itinerary_items twice with the same items in one response. The first call successfully added 5 items. The second call (after the text response) attempted the same 5 items and was correctly rejected with 'already_exists' status (skipped_count: 5). This is wasteful but not harmful.
ux_issues.suggestion: After the tool loop completes and the AI generates its text response, prevent a second tool call with identical items. The AI should recognize the items were already created in the same turn.

ux_issues.description: The final text response in Test 2 contained duplicate content — the AI's initial substantive response (from the first tool loop iteration) was concatenated with a shorter follow-up (from the second iteration after the duplicate tool call was rejected). The user sees both, which is redundant.
ux_issues.suggestion: When a tool call returns 'already_exists', the AI should not repeat content it already provided. Consider suppressing or deduplicating text across tool loop iterations.

ux_issues.description: Trip theme was auto-tagged as 'food' only, despite the trip description mentioning wildlife, nature, adventure, and family. The theme tagging missed the dominant themes of the trip.
ux_issues.suggestion: Review the theme tagging logic to ensure multi-theme trips are correctly classified. This trip should have been tagged with at least 'nature', 'family', and 'adventure'.

ai_behavior_issues.issue: The first message in selection mode (a throwaway 'Hello, I want to plan a trip' message with a different session) triggered only clarifying questions and no trip creation. While this is acceptable for a vague message, it shows the AI errs on the side of asking rather than acting. The full persona message immediately triggered create_trip, which is correct.
ai_behavior_issues.context: First warm-up message was vague, second message with full family details correctly triggered create_trip with Costa Rica destination.

ai_behavior_issues.issue: The AI proactively called create_itinerary_items during the trip creation response (Test 1) without the user explicitly asking for an itinerary. While this is actually good behavior per the system prompt ('always create itinerary items'), it created only 6 items for a 10-day trip, leaving days 6, 9, and 10 empty. The follow-up in Test 2 filled in more items but gaps remained.
ai_behavior_issues.context: After all messages, the itinerary covers days 1-8 with 13 items total. Days 9 and 10 remain empty. A 10-day trip should ideally have all 10 days populated, even if some are marked as free/rest days.

usefulness_evaluation.narrative: Exceptional performance across all test vectors. Trip creation was immediate and correct — the AI created the trip with 'CR' destination, captured all family details (kids' names, ages, interests, Noah's swimming inability), and began building the itinerary proactively. Context retention was perfect: the AI never asked where the family was going after trip creation, referenced both kids by name throughout, and consistently adapted recommendations for Noah's safety. The expert handoff triggered naturally when activities were discussed, bringing in 'Elena' (nature + family expert) who provided age-appropriate breakdown of activities with specific minimum age requirements. Safety information was outstanding — specific hospital/clinic names per region, realistic drive times to medical facilities, and actionable water safety tips (life vest rule, specific beach recommendations, hot springs floor warnings). Driving logistics were detailed and realistic: 3-4 hour drive times between areas, 4x4 necessity explained, car seat laws mentioned, and even a kid entertainment strategy provided. Companion mode was concise and practical — three restaurant recommendations with a Spanish allergy phrase for Noah's nut allergy. The only gap was incomplete itinerary coverage (days 9-10 empty) and duplicate content from a redundant tool call. Overall, this would genuinely help a family plan a safe and exciting Costa Rica vacation.
